calibration.keySource: moherp.com (rivers.moherp.org) community trip reports + date-based historic flow percentiles for gauge 07058000, cross-checked against NWS AHPS flood stages and USGS peak-flow rating. IMPORTANT: moherp has NO fixed Poor/Low/Good/High/Flood cfs cutoffs for this gauge (it shows an 'Unknown/Potential' rating at higher levels and derives floatability from observed community trip reports plus the historic median for the date). Anchors for too_low through optimal_max are grounded in real observed community float ratings; high and dangerous are LOW-confidence inferences because no published recreational cfs cutoff exists for this creek.

too_low.method: Lowest moherp community trip still rated floatable ('Low') was 242 cfs; mid-July historic median is ~246 cfs and P25 ~198 cfs. Below roughly 200 cfs (near P25) the creek enters moherp's 'Poor - too low to float comfortably, most/all riffles drag or must be portaged' territory. No single published cutoff; anchored just below the lowest observed floatable flow and near the P25 statistic.

low.method: Direct moherp community observation: a trip at 242 cfs (gage 4.11 ft) was rated 'Low' - 'low and hot, but nice day for multiple swim breaks.' Mid-July historic median discharge is ~246 cfs. Represents low-but-floatable (expect dragging on most riffles).

optimal_min.method: Boundary between moherp 'Low' (observed 242 cfs) and 'Good' (observed 309, 331, 335, 341 cfs all rated 'Good/ideal'). ~300 cfs is the lowest flow consistently rated 'Good' (boats drag on fewer than one-quarter of riffles). Four independent community trip reports support this.

optimal_max.method: Highest moherp community flow still rated 'Good' was 582 cfs (gage 5.04 ft), described as 'a wider river without much strainer or hydraulic threat.' The true upper edge of ideal is likely somewhat higher but is unobserved; anchored at ~600 cfs as the highest confirmed comfortable float.

high.method: INFERRED - no published cfs cutoff. Onset of elevated/pushy water: above the highest observed comfortable float (582 cfs) and above the mid-July P90 (~518 cfs), rising toward P95. Corresponds to moherp's qualitative 'High' category ('high and potentially dangerous, especially for the young/inexperienced; normal obstacles hard to navigate; canoe travel may not be advisable'). Treat as a caution flag, not a hard line. Low confidence.

dangerous.method: INFERRED conservative danger onset - no published recreational cutoff. Anchored at the mid-July P95 (~1750 cfs, a flow exceeded only ~5% of the time in that season) because this low-volume, willow/strainer-choked creek 'becomes dangerous in high water' with rising willow-entrapment risk (southwestpaddler/McCord; floatmissouri). NOTE the OFFICIAL hard flood reference is far higher: NWS flood stage at Tecumseh (07058000) is 16 ft, which USGS annual peaks show equals ~18,000 cfs (action stage 14 ft; major 25 ft; record 33.07 ft / 111,000 cfs in 2017). Recreational danger begins well below official flood stage - after local rain, water rises and muddies fast, so visual inspection is the real go/no-go, not a single number. Low confidence.

calibration.notes: PRIMARY GAUGE CORRECTION: the originally supplied ID 07056700 is 'Buffalo River near Harriet, AR' (a different river in Arkansas) - verified against USGS twice. The correct gauge is 07058000 'Bryant Creek near Tecumseh, MO' (Ozark County; drainage 570 sq mi; lat 36.6272, lon -92.3061; datum 573.56 ft NAVD88; ~70+ yr record back to the early 1950s), which reports both discharge 00060 (cfs) and gage height 00065 (ft). moherp keys this gauge on cfs, so the ladder is in cfs. moherp does NOT publish fixed Poor/Low/Good/High/Flood cfs breakpoints for this gauge; it rates conditions from community trip reports and whether current flow is at/above the historic median for the date. Trip-report calibration observed (all on 07058000): 242 cfs=Low; 309/331/335/341 cfs=Good; 582 cfs=Good/comfortable. Mid-July date-percentiles: P05 141, P25 198, P50(median) 246, P75 358, P90 518, P95 1750 cfs. too_low/low/optimal are well-grounded; HIGH and DANGEROUS are low-confidence inferences (no published recreational cfs exists) and should be treated as conservative caution flags plus a reminder to visually inspect after rain. NWS flood categories (ft): action 14, minor/flood 16, moderate 20, major 25.

secondaryGauges.gaugeName: North Fork River near Tecumseh, MO
secondaryGauges.relationship: The North Fork of the White River, which Bryant Creek joins ~0.5 mi above Norfork Lake near Tecumseh. Larger and more strongly spring-fed than Bryant; useful for planning combined/extended floats onto the North Fork and for reading regional conditions. Not a substitute for the Bryant gauge - Bryant runs lower and flashier.

sections.name: Vera Cruz to Highway 95 (Bell Bridge) - Upper Bryant
sections.putIn: Vera Cruz Access (Hwy AB low-water bridge)
sections.difficulty: Class I-II (upper reach: narrow, frequently choked with deadfall and willow strainers; higher-water only, extreme caution; often not floatable in summer without wading/dragging riffles)

sections.name: Highway 95 (Bell Bridge) to Sycamore / Hodgson Mill
sections.takeOut: Sycamore Access (MDC), below Hwy 181 at Hodgson Mill

sections.name: Sycamore / Hodgson Mill to Warren Bridge
sections.putIn: Sycamore Access (MDC), below Hwy 181 at Hodgson Mill
sections.takeOut: Warren Bridge (County Rd 328 / Hwys FF & H area)
sections.difficulty: Class I (traditionally the most popular reach; passes 'The Narrows' sharp bend near mile 29)

sections.name: Warren Bridge to Florence C. Cook (Cook's Landing)
sections.takeOut: Florence C. Cook Access / Cook's Landing (County Rd 308)

sections.name: Florence Cook to North Fork Confluence (Tecumseh)
sections.putIn: Florence C. Cook Access / Cook's Landing (County Rd 308)
sections.takeOut: North Fork White River confluence / Tecumseh (Hwy 160 area, Norfork Lake backwater)
sections.difficulty: Class I, then flatwater (lower end is Norfork Lake backwater when the lake is up)

metadata.difficultyRating: Class I (occasional Class I-II riffles; upper reach above Hwy 95 more technical)
metadata.description: Bryant Creek is a wild, spring-influenced Ozark float that flows south about 42.6 miles from near Vera Cruz/Vanzant in Douglas County to its confluence with the North Fork of the White River near Tecumseh in Ozark County, just above Norfork Lake. It is a gentle Class I stream (a few Class I-II riffles) with clear pools, bluffs, gravel bars, willow jungles and rock shoals, prized for smallmouth bass and goggle-eye fishing. It is less spring-fed and lower-volume than the neighboring North Fork - narrower, with more obstacles and strainers - so it drags in drought and can turn dangerous quickly after local rain. It pairs naturally with the North Fork of the White River, into which it empties, letting paddlers extend a trip onto the larger, more reliably spring-fed river. Gradient averages ~5.8 ft/mi. The most popular reach is Sycamore/Hodgson Mill to Warren Bridge.
metadata.nhdReachHint: Bryant Creek (Douglas & Ozark counties, MO), forming north of MO-76 near Vera Cruz/Vanzant and flowing south ~42.6 mi to its confluence with the North Fork White River near Tecumseh, ~0.5 mi above Norfork Lake. USGS gauge 07058000 is near the mouth at Tecumseh.
metadata.managingAuthority: Missouri Department of Conservation (MDC) - operates the Sycamore Access at Hodgson Mill (the only MDC Bryant access currently open); US Army Corps of Engineers manages the lower confluence/Norfork Lake area (Tecumseh); surrounding uplands include Mark Twain National Forest (USFS).
metadata.accessLaw: Missouri: the public may float, wade and fish navigable/floatable streams within the ordinary high-water mark, but streambeds and banks are frequently privately owned. Legal access is limited to public points and road/bridge rights-of-way (MDC/USACE accesses, low-water bridges); crossing or portaging over private banks requires landowner permission. Several Bryant accesses were damaged in the 2017 flood - Warren Bridge and Cook's Landing are officially closed though used informally - so confirm current access status before a trip.

hazards.name: Low-water bridges (strainers in high water)
hazards.description: Multiple low-water bridges cross Bryant Creek: Monastery (Hwy OO), Bertha Ford (Co Rd N-345), Highway 95/Bell School, Hodgson Mill (Hwy 181), and Warren Bridge. They serve as access points at normal flow but become dangerous strainers when water is up - scout and portage; do not run a submerged or overtopped bridge.

hazards.name: Hodgson Mill (Highway 181 low-water bridge) - NOT a runnable dam
hazards.description: Historic Hodgson Mill was powered by a spring (~24 million gallons/day), NOT by a dam across Bryant Creek - there is no low-head dam here to run or portage. The hazard is the Highway 181 low-water bridge (dangerous in high water). The MDC Sycamore Access sits just below the bridge; watch for loose sand and deep gravel at the access (vehicle bogging).

hazards.name: Upper reach strainers (above Hwy 95)
hazards.description: Above the Bell School low-water bridge (Hwy 95) the creek is narrow and often choked with deadfall and willow strainers; the upper reach can be paddled but extreme caution is urged (entrapment risk). Frequently marginal / requires dragging in summer.

hazards.name: Willow jungles, sweepers and sharp bends
hazards.description: Willow jungles, rock shoals and sharp bends throughout (including 'The Narrows' near mile 29, a ~2-mile bend spanning only ~0.2 mi straight-line). Willow-entrapment risk increases as flows rise.

hazards.name: Flash / high-water danger
hazards.description: Low-volume, spring-influenced creek that rises and muddies quickly after local rain and can become dangerous fast. Visually inspect before putting in after significant rainfall. NWS flood stages at Tecumseh gauge 07058000: action 14 ft, flood 16 ft (~18,000 cfs), moderate 20 ft, major 25 ft; record 33.07 ft / ~111,000 cfs (Apr 2017).

hazards.name: Low water / dragging in drought
hazards.description: Below the optimal range (roughly under ~250-300 cfs at gauge 07058000) expect significant dragging and possible portaging over riffles, especially in summer and on the upper reaches.

hazards.name: Norfork Lake backwater at the mouth
hazards.description: The lowest ~2-3 miles near Tecumseh become Norfork Lake backwater/flatwater when the lake is up; expect a paddle-out and possible motorboat traffic. Some low accesses (e.g., Bridges Creek / Stump Hole) can be submerged when the lake is high.

hazards.name: Access closures / uncertainty (post-2017 flood)
hazards.description: The 2017 flood destroyed or damaged several put-ins/take-outs. Warren Bridge and Cook's Landing are officially closed (used informally); Tecumseh Park (USACE) is closed but foot-accessible. Confirm access status and parking before committing to a shuttle.
